findings.description: offset = page * page_size with a documented 1-based page silently skips the entire first page (page 1 returns rows 21-40), so the first user is invisible to every caller — the math should be (page - 1) * page_size.
User-supplied name_pattern is compiled and run as a raw regex, so a catastrophic-backtracking pattern like (a+)+$ is a trivial one-line ReDoS denial of service.
No SQL LIMIT/OFFSET: every matching user is fetched, dict-ified, and sorted in Python before slicing, so 'pagination' loads the whole table into memory and does not scale.
matcher.search(u['display_name']) assumes display_name is always a non-null string; a NULL column raises TypeError and aborts the entire search.
Regex name filtering runs in Python after fetchall() rather than in SQL, defeating any DB index and coupling correctness to the in-memory load problem.
page_size is clamped to MAX but never floored; page_size=0 yields a permanently empty window and a negative page_size produces nonsensical slice behavior, with no validation.
Negative or zero page is never validated and flows into offset, producing negative offsets and Python wrap-around slicing — silently wrong windows instead of an error.
